title: Everyone's Chasing the Next Nvidia. CoreWeave Already Has a $100 Billion Head Start.
subtitle: The market is pricing CoreWeave like a distressed cloud company. The fundamentals say it's a purpose-built AI factory with a structural moat, a strategic patron in NVIDIA, and $100 billion in contracted backlog.
summary: CoreWeave trades at $73.21 with a $39.9B market cap — roughly half its 52-week high. But while shorts feast on the 45% slide, the company has quietly locked in $100B in take-or-pay contracts, landed a $2B strategic investment from NVIDIA, and built purpose-built AI infrastructure that hyperscalers can't replicate. This is the AI factory thesis in its purest form.

links.label: Meta expands CoreWeave deal to $35B through 2032 (Bloomberg) →

videos.title: CoreWeave CEO on Nvidia, AI, and Building the Cloud | Barron's

bodyHtml:
Let's talk about the most misunderstood stock on the Nasdaq right now.

CoreWeave — CRWV — closed Friday at $73.21, carrying a $39.9 billion market cap that's barely half of what it was five months ago. The shorts are feasting. The headlines are brutal. On the surface, this looks like a company in freefall.

Here's what that narrative misses: a $100 billion number that changes everything.

That's not a projection or some aspirational target. It's contracted take-or-pay backlog. Signed paper. Committed dollars from the most demanding AI buyers on the planet — Meta at $35 billion through 2032, OpenAI at $22.4 billion. No cloud company in history has walked into a year with this level of revenue visibility.

The story everyone's telling is about the 45% slide from the $153.20 high. The story nobody's telling is what CoreWeave actually built while the noise machine churned. This is not a traditional cloud provider trying to be AWS with GPU rentals. CoreWeave is an AI factory — purpose-built for massive-scale parallel GPU workloads, with infrastructure hyperscalers cannot replicate without gutting their data centers.

The numbers back this up. CoreWeave did $5.13 billion in revenue in FY2025 — 168% year-over-year growth. FY2026 guidance sits at $12 to $13 billion, another 134 to 153% jump. Adjusted EBITDA margins hover around 60%, revealing real operational leverage underneath. The $1.17 billion GAAP net loss is real, but it's almost entirely $2.45 billion in depreciation and $1.23 billion in interest — the cost of building the infrastructure generating that revenue.

The structural moat comes down to three compounding advantages competitors can't replicate fast.

Start with NVIDIA. In January, Jensen Huang's team invested $2 billion at $87.20 per share, making NVIDIA the second-largest shareholder. This is not a vendor relationship — it's a strategic alliance. CoreWeave gets priority access to the scarcest asset in AI hardware: next-gen GPUs including Blackwell successors and Vera Rubin, before anyone else. No hyperscaler has this level of co-development with NVIDIA. They keep competitive distance. CoreWeave gets the keys.

Then there's scale. CoreWeave is deploying $30 to $35 billion in CapEx in 2026 alone — more than the entire market cap of most neocloud competitors. They've got 850 megawatts active, targeting over 5 gigawatts by 2030 across 32 data centers. This scale creates self-reinforcing advantages: utility-grade power pricing, preferential GPU allocation from NVIDIA's fabs, and the institutional trust to win $20 billion contracts. Smaller neoclouds operate at a fraction of this scale.

And the architecture itself. CoreWeave runs Kubernetes-native, InfiniBand-connected clusters engineered for the density of 1,000-plus GPU configurations. Hyperscalers retrofit GPU instances onto infrastructure built for databases and web servers. CoreWeave starts from scratch with AI as the single priority — delivering 2x to 5x better price-performance, an advantage that widens as AI shifts from training to massive-scale inference.

Meta's $35 billion expansion tells you everything. That commitment is not for training runs — it's for production inference powering AI features across Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp. That's the endgame. Inference at planetary scale is the new frontier, and CoreWeave is engineered specifically for it.

The bear case deserves your full attention. Debt-to-equity sits at 739%. Free cash flow was negative $7.25 billion in FY2025 and will deepen further with $30 billion-plus in CapEx. Customer concentration is real — Meta and OpenAI represent the vast majority of that backlog. And NVIDIA dependency cuts both ways: if that alliance ever fractures, the thesis gets complicated overnight.

But structural moats are hardest to see when everyone is panicking. CoreWeave trades at 11.7 times trailing EV-to-revenue. Analyst consensus is Buy, with a mean target of $140.85 — roughly 92% upside. Short interest sits at 27% of float, a massive squeeze catalyst if fundamentals break through the noise.

This stock isn't for the faint of heart. The GAAP losses keep coming as long as the company builds faster than it earns. But for investors who see what's actually here — an AI factory with a structural moat, a strategic patron in NVIDIA, and a backlog that would make any hyperscaler jealous — the current price might be the opportunity of the cycle.

The market is pricing CoreWeave like a distressed cloud company. The fundamentals say it's something else entirely.

Disclosure: The Signal holds no position in CRWV. Positions may change. This is not financial advice.
